title: Navigating the Filesystem
description:
## Navigating the Linux Filesystem

Everything in Linux is organised under a single root directory, `/`. Paths either start from the root (**absolute**, e.g. `/etc/hostname`) or from where you currently are (**relative**, e.g. `logs/app.log`).

### Core commands

| Command | What it does |
|---|---|
| `pwd` | Print the **current** working directory |
| `ls` | List directory contents (`ls -l` for details, `ls -a` to include hidden files) |
| `cd <dir>` | Change directory (`cd ..` goes up one level, `cd ~` goes home) |
| `cat <file>` | Print a file's contents |
| `mkdir <dir>` | Create a directory |
| `touch <file>` | Create an empty file (or update its timestamp) |

### A few useful ideas

- **Hidden files** start with a dot (`.bashrc`). They are skipped by `ls` unless you pass `-a`.
- **`~`** is a shortcut for your home directory (here, `/root`).
- Tab-completion saves typing — start a name and press `Tab`.

When you are comfortable with these, mark this lesson complete and move on to the exercises.
